case.caption: Wells v. Drayton Holdings, Inc.
cause_of_action: wrongful termination in violation of public policy
case.summary: FLSA retaliation and wrongful termination action. Wells, a Senior Operations Analyst, alleges Drayton fired her for complaining about unpaid overtime. On Drayton's Rule 56 motion, the court denied summary judgment on the FLSA retaliation and wrongful termination claims (finding a triable temporal-proximity issue tied to a disputed date when HR received Wells's complaint) and granted it on the implied contract claim. Trial pending.

parties.name: United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania

events.label: Wells hired as Senior Operations Analyst
events.detail: Drayton hires Wells as a Senior Operations Analyst, reporting to Operations Director Michael Chu.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Start of employment relationship at issue

events.label: Wells's claimed overtime period (~53 hrs/wk)
events.detail: Wells estimates she worked an average of 53 hours per week from February through October 2022 to absorb the workload of two departed analysts. The number of hours is contested (see Dispute d_hours_worked).
events.jurisdictional_significance: Underlying conduct that gave rise to the FLSA overtime complaint

events.label: Drayton's recorded hours (40-44 hrs/wk)
events.detail: Drayton's timekeeping records show Wells clocking 40-44 hours per week during the same Feb-Oct 2022 period.

events.label: Two client complaints received
events.detail: Drayton asserts two client complaints were received in July and August 2022, which it cites as the basis for the later PIP.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Drayton's asserted non-retaliatory basis for the PIP; relevant to pretext

events.label: Wells emails Chu re uncompensated overtime
events.detail: Wells sends Chu an email raising concerns about uncompensated overtime, attaching a spreadsheet of her hours worked. This is the alleged protected activity under the FLSA.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Alleged protected activity under 29 U.S.C. § 215(a)(3); start point for temporal-proximity analysis

events.label: HR receives complaint (Wells's version)
events.detail: Wells contends Chu forwarded her Sept 8 email to HR Director Elaine Morris that same day. If credited, the gap to the Oct 14 PIP is approximately five weeks.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Wells's start point for retaliation temporal-proximity analysis; ~5-week gap to PIP under Williams v. Phila. Hous. Auth., 380 F.3d 751 (3d Cir. 2004)

events.label: HR receives complaint (Drayton's version)
events.detail: Morris testified she did not receive any forwarded complaint until Oct 3, 2022, when Chu first raised it in a meeting. If credited, the gap to the Oct 14 PIP shrinks to eleven days.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Drayton's start point for retaliation temporal-proximity analysis; 11-day gap to PIP

events.label: PIP issued
events.detail: Wells receives a performance improvement plan citing alleged deficiencies in client communication. Wells contends the PIP is pretextual, pointing to her 'exceeds expectations' 2021 annual review.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Alleged adverse employment action; endpoint for temporal-proximity analysis

events.label: Wells terminated
events.detail: Drayton terminates Wells's employment. The termination letter cites 'failure to satisfy PIP milestones.'
events.jurisdictional_significance: Adverse employment action giving rise to retaliation and wrongful termination claims

events.label: Complaint filed
events.detail: Wells files her Complaint asserting (1) FLSA retaliation under 29 U.S.C. § 215(a)(3); (2) wrongful termination in violation of public policy; and (3) breach of implied contract based on Drayton's progressive discipline policy.

events.label: Answer filed
events.detail: Drayton files its Answer to the Complaint.

events.label: Scheduling order entered
events.detail: Court enters scheduling order, setting fact discovery to close on April 1, 2024.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Sets discovery cutoff and case management deadlines

events.label: Discovery deadline extended
events.detail: Parties stipulate to extend the discovery deadline to May 31, 2024.

events.label: Fact discovery closes
events.detail: Extended fact discovery deadline; parties had taken 12 depositions and produced approximately 8,400 pages.

events.label: Drayton MSJ filed
events.detail: Drayton files Motion for Summary Judgment under Rule 56 as to all three claims.

events.label: Wells's opposition filed
events.detail: Wells files her opposition to summary judgment.

events.label: Drayton's reply filed
events.detail: Drayton files its reply in support of summary judgment.

events.label: Oral argument on MSJ
events.detail: Court hears oral argument on Drayton's Motion for Summary Judgment.

events.label: MSJ ruling (denied in part, granted in part)
events.detail: Memorandum Opinion of Judge Beresford: DENIES summary judgment on FLSA retaliation (a reasonable jury could find temporal proximity under either party's version of when HR received the complaint) and on wrongful termination; GRANTS summary judgment on breach of implied contract.
events.jurisdictional_significance: Disposes of implied contract claim; retaliation and wrongful termination claims proceed to trial

relationships.detail: Wells's theory: Chu forwarded the Sept 8 email to HR the same day
Drayton's theory: HR did not learn of the complaint until Chu raised it at an Oct 3 meeting
Wells's theory: ~5-week gap supports inference of causation under Williams v. Phila. Hous. Auth.
Drayton's version yields an 11-day gap, which the court found 'even more probative' of causation
Drayton's asserted non-retaliatory basis for the PIP
Termination letter cites 'failure to satisfy PIP milestones'
Stipulated extension supersedes the April 1, 2024 discovery cutoff
Wells's declared ~53 hrs/wk vs. Drayton's timekeeping showing 40-44 hrs/wk for the same period

disputes.subject: Date Drayton's HR department received Wells's overtime complaint

versions.basis: Wells Dep. 42:18-43:7 — Chu forwarded the Sept 8 email to HR Director Morris the same day
Morris Dep. 31:4-19 and Ex. D-7 (Morris's calendar) — Morris first learned of the complaint when Chu raised it at an Oct 3, 2022 meeting

disputes.materiality: Material to the temporal-proximity element of Wells's FLSA retaliation claim. Wells's version yields a ~5-week gap between protected activity and the PIP (sufficient under Williams v. Phila. Hous. Auth., 380 F.3d 751 (3d Cir. 2004)); Drayton's version yields an 11-day gap (even more probative). Court found that under either version a reasonable jury could find temporal proximity, so the dispute did not prevent denial of summary judgment but remains for the jury.

disputes.subject: Number of hours Wells worked per week, Feb-Oct 2022

versions.basis: Wells Decl. ¶¶ 8-11 — average of 53 hours per week
Chu Decl. ¶ 14 (Ex. D-3) — timekeeping records show 40-44 hours per week

disputes.materiality: Court expressly did not resolve this discrepancy on summary judgment; underlies the FLSA overtime grievance that forms the predicate protected activity
